title: Introduction
text:
Introduction
Last updated: 2026-07-20
This book documents CanadaLogin metrics and the tables they are calculated from.
It has two chapters:

The Metrics Cookbook defines commonly used metrics, including their calculation logic and example SQL queries.
The Data Catalog describes the underlying tables, field by field.

title: Additional Notes
text:
A sign-in count measures events. One person can sign in multiple times.
Use sign-ins when the question is about volume or system usage.
Use unique users when the question is about reach.

title: Additional Notes

title: Additional Notes
section: Daily unique users are not the same as period unique users
text:
Daily unique users are not the same as period unique users
Summing daily unique users gives a measure of user-days, not distinct people over the full period.
For distinct people over a longer period, use a cumulative unique user field such as mtd_unique_users or ytd_unique_users.

title: Additional Notes
section: Metrics can be reported over different timespans
text:
Metrics can be reported over different timespans
Most metrics are meaningful over more than one window. The three standard reporting windows are month-to-date, year-to-date, and since the April 22, 2026 public launch. How you select a window depends on the kind of metric.
Additive metrics (sign-ins, MFA attempts, success rates, averages) are computed by aggregating daily rows, so the window is just the date bounds in the WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '<start>' AND DATE '<end>' clause. The same query covers any window; only the start date changes:

Month-to-date: first of the current month (for example, 2026-06-01)
Year-to-date: 2026-01-01
Since launch: 2026-04-22

Set the end date to the reporting date.
Cumulative unique-user metrics cannot be derived by filtering a date range, because summing daily unique_users counts user-days, not distinct people. Instead, pick the pre-computed column that matches the window and take its latest value:

Month-to-date: mtd_unique_users
Year-to-date: ytd_unique_users

Never sum these cumulative columns across rows.
There is no cumulative since-launch column. The since_apr22_unique_users field that previously provided it has been removed: the cumulative count is computed by the IBM Verify API, which sees only the most recent 366.5 days of source data, so a since-launch total silently truncates once launch is more than a year in the past. The mtd_ and ytd_ windows are unaffected because they fall inside that horizon.
This limit does not affect the additive metrics above. Those are aggregated from the daily rows we store on our side, which are retained beyond a year, so a date-bounded since-launch window over an additive metric stays correct however far back it reaches. See the data retention note in the catalog.

title: Additional Notes
section: Weighted averages should be used for call centre averages
text:
Weighted averages should be used for call centre averages
When calculating average call length or average delay across multiple weeks, use a weighted average based on calls_answered.
A simple average of weekly averages may give too much influence to low-volume weeks.

title: Additional Notes
section: Internal applications should usually be excluded from external adoption reporting
text:
Internal applications should usually be excluded from external adoption reporting
The following applications are currently treated as internal:

Flow Application
GCS Migration Solution
GC Sign In - Profile Management App

Whether to exclude them depends on the reporting question.

title: About
text:
This cookbook documents commonly used CanadaLogin metrics, including their definitions, plain-language alternate names, calculation logic, and example SQL queries.
The cookbook is organized by data source:

IBM Verify
Call Centre
Platform
Cross-source / interpretive metrics (planned)

Each metric includes:

a description of what it measures and when to use it
plain-language alternative names
the calculation logic
an example query, shown in both SQL and R
interpretation notes, where relevant

The companion Data Catalog describes the underlying tables that these metrics are calculated from, field by field.


Use this cookbook as a reference when building dashboards, reports, briefing materials, or ad hoc analyses.
For each metric, confirm:

Does it measure what I think it does?
What table and columns are used?
Should I apply any filters?
Is this metric a count, a rate, a cumulative value, or an average?




All example SQL targets Amazon Athena (Trino dialect). Constructs such as DATE '...' literals, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (...), and REGEXP_REPLACE follow Trino semantics.
In reporting language, prefer sign-in over login. The underlying IBM Verify field may be called total_logins, but “sign-ins” is preferred (and often enforced) when talking about CanadaLogin.
Where applicable, internal applications are excluded from external-facing adoption reporting. As of June 3, 2026, the current internal application exclusion list is:

Flow Application
GCS Migration Solution
GC Sign In - Profile Management App




This cookbook covers calculated metrics. Metrics already present in a table without any calculation do not need to be included, although they can be if they need clarification.
The initial version focuses on:

IBM Verify application sign-in metrics
call centre activity metrics
call centre topic metrics

Cross-source metrics comparing sign-in activity and call volume are planned for a future revision.

title: Call Centre
text:
Metrics in this section are calculated from call centre activity and topic data.
Call centre metrics are useful for understanding support demand, service responsiveness, caller wait times, average call duration, and common reasons for contact. The source data is weekly, so these metrics are typically reported at a weekly level or aggregated across weeks.
The relevant call centre tables are:
date_range_start and date_range_end are stored as text, so date-range filters cast them to DATE before comparing to date literals.

title: Call Centre
text:
Activity Metrics
Metrics in this section describe call centre operations, sourced from call_centre.weekly_activity_report.

Calls Accepted
The number of calls accepted by the call centre during a reporting period.
This metric is useful for tracking call centre demand and understanding how contact volume changes over time.
Alternative names:

calls received
accepted calls
weekly call volume
call centre demand
number of calls handled by the queue

Calculation. Use calls_accepted by reporting period, or sum across periods for a total.

SQLR


By week:
SELECT
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    CAST(calls_accepted AS BIGINT) AS calls_accepted
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
ORDER BY date_range_start;
Total over a period:
SELECT
    SUM(CAST(calls_accepted AS BIGINT)) AS total_calls_accepted
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';


By week:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  arrange(date_range_start) |>
  select(
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    calls_accepted
  )
Total over a period:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    total_calls_accepted = sum(calls_accepted, na.rm = TRUE)
  )



Interpretation notes. Calls accepted may not be identical to calls answered, depending on call centre definitions and routing logic.


Calls Answered
The number of calls answered by call centre agents during a reporting period.
This metric is also used as a weighting variable when calculating weighted average call length and weighted average delay.
Alternative names:

answered calls
calls handled
agent-answered calls
completed calls
calls taken by agents

Calculation. Use calls_answered by reporting period, or sum across periods for a total.

SQLR


By week:
SELECT
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    CAST(calls_answered AS BIGINT) AS calls_answered
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
ORDER BY date_range_start;
Total over a period:
SELECT
    SUM(CAST(calls_answered AS BIGINT)) AS total_calls_answered
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';


By week:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  arrange(date_range_start) |>
  select(
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    calls_answered
  )
Total over a period:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    total_calls_answered = sum(calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE)
  )



Interpretation notes. Calls answered is commonly used as the weighting field when combining weekly average call length or delay values.


Average Call Length
The average length of answered calls.
The overall average call length is calculated as a weighted mean of avg_call_length_seconds, weighted by calls_answered. This is preferable to a simple average of weekly averages because weeks with more answered calls should contribute more to the overall result.
Alternative names:

average call duration
average handle time
average length of call
typical call length
average time on call

Calculation. Calculate a weighted average of weekly average call length, weighted by the number of answered calls.

SQLR


Weighted average over a period:
SELECT
    SUM(
        CAST(avg_call_length_seconds AS DOUBLE)
        * CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)
    )
    / NULLIF(SUM(CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)), 0) AS weighted_avg_call_length_seconds
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';
By week:
SELECT
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    CAST(avg_call_length_seconds AS DOUBLE) AS avg_call_length_seconds,
    CAST(calls_answered AS BIGINT) AS calls_answered
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
ORDER BY date_range_start;


Weighted average over a period:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    weighted_avg_call_length_seconds =
      sum(avg_call_length_seconds * calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
By week:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  arrange(date_range_start) |>
  select(
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    avg_call_length_seconds,
    calls_answered
  )



Interpretation notes. Use a weighted average rather than a simple average of weekly averages. A simple average may give too much influence to low-volume weeks.


Average Delay
The average delay before calls are answered.
This metric is useful for understanding caller wait times and call centre responsiveness. Like average call length, it should be calculated as a weighted mean to avoid low-volume weeks distorting the result.
Alternative names:

average wait time
average delay before answer
caller wait time
time to answer
average queue delay

Calculation. Calculate a weighted average of weekly average delay, weighted by the number of answered calls.

SQLR


Weighted average over a period:
SELECT
    SUM(
        CAST(avg_delay_seconds AS DOUBLE)
        * CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)
    )
    / NULLIF(SUM(CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)), 0) AS weighted_avg_delay_seconds
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';
By week:
SELECT
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    CAST(avg_delay_seconds AS DOUBLE) AS avg_delay_seconds,
    CAST(calls_answered AS BIGINT) AS calls_answered
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
ORDER BY date_range_start;


Weighted average over a period:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    weighted_avg_delay_seconds =
      sum(avg_delay_seconds * calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
By week:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  arrange(date_range_start) |>
  select(
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    avg_delay_seconds,
    calls_answered
  )



Interpretation notes. Average delay should be interpreted alongside call volume. A low-volume week can produce unusual wait times that should not dominate the overall result.


Abandonment Rate
The proportion of accepted calls that were abandoned before being answered.
This metric is useful for understanding how often callers give up while waiting. It is closely tied to wait times: rising delay often drives rising abandonment.
Alternative names:

abandon rate
call abandonment rate
drop rate
proportion of calls abandoned

Calculation. Divide the sum of calls_abandoned by the sum of calls_accepted over the selected reporting period. Summing the raw counts gives a volume-weighted rate; do not average the weekly calls_abandoned_pct column.

SQLR


SELECT
    SUM(CAST(calls_abandoned AS DOUBLE))
        / NULLIF(SUM(CAST(calls_accepted AS DOUBLE)), 0) AS abandonment_rate
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';


tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    abandonment_rate =
      sum(calls_abandoned, na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(calls_accepted, na.rm = TRUE)
  )



Interpretation notes. The rate is a proportion between 0 and 1. Read it together with average delay; a spike in abandonment usually coincides with longer waits.


Answer Rate
The proportion of accepted calls that were answered by an agent.
This metric is the complement of abandonment and any other non-answered outcomes. It summarizes how much of incoming demand the call centre actually served.
Alternative names:

answer ratio
proportion of calls answered
pickup rate
calls answered rate

Calculation. Divide the sum of calls_answered by the sum of calls_accepted over the selected reporting period.

SQLR


SELECT
    SUM(CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE))
        / NULLIF(SUM(CAST(calls_accepted AS DOUBLE)), 0) AS answer_rate
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';


tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    answer_rate =
      sum(calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(calls_accepted, na.rm = TRUE)
  )



Interpretation notes. The rate is a proportion between 0 and 1. A high answer rate with rising delay can still indicate strain: calls are answered eventually, but callers wait longer to reach an agent.


Service Level (Answered Within 60s)
The proportion of calls answered within 60 seconds.
This metric is a standard call centre service-level measure of how quickly the queue is cleared. The source data stores only the weekly proportion (pct_answered_within_60s), not the underlying within-60s count, so combine weeks with a weighted average rather than re-deriving from counts.
Alternative names:

service level
SLA
grade of service
percent answered within 60 seconds
speed of answer

Calculation. Calculate a weighted average of pct_answered_within_60s, weighted by calls_answered.

SQLR


SELECT
    SUM(
        CAST(pct_answered_within_60s AS DOUBLE)
        * CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)
    )
    / NULLIF(SUM(CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)), 0) AS service_level_60s
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';


tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    service_level_60s =
      sum(pct_answered_within_60s * calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE)
  )



Interpretation notes. The result is a proportion between 0 and 1. Because only the weekly proportion is stored, the weighted average is an approximation of the true within-60s rate, but it is far closer than a simple average of weekly values.


Average Handle Time
The average total time agents spend per call, including any after-call wrap-up.
avg_handle_time_seconds covers more than avg_call_length_seconds: it adds wrap-up work after the caller disconnects. Like the other averages, combine weeks with a weighted mean.
Alternative names:

AHT
average handling time
total handle time per call
average talk plus wrap time

Calculation. Calculate a weighted average of avg_handle_time_seconds, weighted by calls_answered.

SQLR


SELECT
    SUM(
        CAST(avg_handle_time_seconds AS DOUBLE)
        * CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)
    )
    / NULLIF(SUM(CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)), 0) AS weighted_avg_handle_time_seconds
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';


tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    weighted_avg_handle_time_seconds =
      sum(avg_handle_time_seconds * calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE)
  )



Interpretation notes. Average handle time is always at least as large as average call length. A widening gap between the two points to growing after-call work.


Calls per Active User
Weekly call centre volume divided by the size of the active user base, so you can compare support demand across weeks even as the user base grows.
Raw call volume moves partly because the number of people using CanadaLogin changes. Normalizing by active users takes that out and leaves how often a typical user needed to phone in on a given day. The figure is scaled to calls per 100 active users to keep it in a readable range.
Alternative names:

call centre contact rate
normalized call volume
support contacts per active user
calls per 100 users

Calculation. For each call centre reporting week, take calls_accepted from call_centre.weekly_activity_report and divide it by the total user-days in that week, then multiply by 100. User-days is the sum of ibm_verify.auth_total_logins.unique_users over the days whose date falls inside the week’s date_range_start to date_range_end, so the numerator and denominator cover the same span of days. This is the same as dividing average daily calls by average daily active users. The two tables store their dates as text, so both sides of the join are cast to DATE.

SQLR


SELECT
    w.date_range_start,
    w.date_range_end,
    CAST(w.calls_accepted AS BIGINT) AS calls_accepted,
    AVG(a.unique_users) AS avg_daily_active_users,
    CAST(w.calls_accepted AS DOUBLE) / SUM(a.unique_users) * 100
        AS calls_per_100_dau
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report AS w
JOIN ibm_verify.auth_total_logins AS a
    ON CAST(a.date AS DATE)
       BETWEEN CAST(w.date_range_start AS DATE) AND CAST(w.date_range_end AS DATE)
GROUP BY w.date_range_start, w.date_range_end, w.calls_accepted
ORDER BY w.date_range_start;


weekly <- tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  select(date_range_start, date_range_end, calls_accepted)

dau <- tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "auth_total_logins")) |>
  select(dau_date = date, unique_users)

weekly |>
  cross_join(dau) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(dau_date) >= as.Date(date_range_start),
    as.Date(dau_date) <= as.Date(date_range_end)
  ) |>
  group_by(date_range_start, date_range_end, calls_accepted) |>
  summarise(
    avg_daily_active_users = mean(unique_users, na.rm = TRUE),
    user_days = sum(unique_users, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    calls_per_100_dau = calls_accepted / user_days * 100
  ) |>
  arrange(date_range_start) |>
  select(
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    calls_accepted,
    avg_daily_active_users,
    calls_per_100_dau
  )



Interpretation notes. Read this as a daily rate: on a typical day that week, roughly this many calls came in per 100 active users. Dividing by user-days keeps calls and users on the same daily footing. The avg_daily_active_users column is the week’s average daily reach, shown for context.
A call centre week runs Sunday to Saturday, from the report’s own date_range_start and date_range_end. The IBM Verify metrics anchor weeks on Monday, so the two do not line up; the join and the denominator follow the call centre boundaries.
A week appears only once daily active user data exists for it, so the series starts near the April 22, 2026 launch. The numerator, calls_accepted, includes test calls, so weeks with heavy test activity read slightly high.

title: Call Centre
text:
Topic Metrics
Metrics in this section describe why people contact the call centre, sourced from call_centre.weekly_topic_dump. Because a single call can generate multiple topic rows, these metrics count topic records rather than distinct calls. Clean statobject before grouping, since it often carries leading, trailing, or repeated whitespace.

Call Topic Share
The percentage of all topic records represented by each call topic.
This is useful for understanding the relative importance of each call driver compared to the overall volume.
Alternative names:

share of calls by topic
percentage of calls by reason
topic distribution
call reason mix
contact driver share

Calculation. Count records by cleaned topic, then divide each topic count by the total count across all topics.

SQLR


WITH topic_counts AS (
    SELECT
        REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(statobject), '\s+', ' ') AS call_topic,
        COUNT(*) AS topic_count
    FROM call_centre.weekly_topic_dump
    GROUP BY REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(statobject), '\s+', ' ')
)

SELECT
    call_topic,
    topic_count,
    CAST(topic_count AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(SUM(topic_count) OVER (), 0) AS topic_share
FROM topic_counts
ORDER BY topic_count DESC;


tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_topic_dump")) |>
  mutate(
    call_topic = str_squish(statobject)
  ) |>
  count(call_topic, name = "topic_count") |>
  mutate(
    topic_share = as.double(topic_count) / sum(topic_count, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(topic_count))



Interpretation notes. Topic share is useful for comparing relative demand across contact reasons. Interpret alongside total call volume.


Top 10 Call Topics
The ten most common call topics in the call centre topic data.
This is useful for quickly identifying the most common reasons people contact the call centre.
Alternative names:

top 10 call reasons
most common call topics
top contact drivers
main reasons for calling
top caller issues

Calculation. Count records by cleaned topic, calculate topic share, and return the top 10 by topic count.

SQLR


WITH topic_counts AS (
    SELECT
        REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(statobject), '\s+', ' ') AS call_topic,
        COUNT(*) AS topic_count
    FROM call_centre.weekly_topic_dump
    GROUP BY REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(statobject), '\s+', ' ')
),

topic_shares AS (
    SELECT
        call_topic,
        topic_count,
        CAST(topic_count AS DOUBLE)
            / NULLIF(SUM(topic_count) OVER (), 0) AS topic_share
    FROM topic_counts
)

SELECT
    call_topic,
    topic_count,
    topic_share
FROM topic_shares
-- sort on topic_count desc, then call_topic asc to break ties deterministically
ORDER BY topic_count DESC, call_topic
LIMIT 10;


tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_topic_dump")) |>
  mutate(
    call_topic = str_squish(statobject)
  ) |>
  count(call_topic, name = "topic_count") |>
  mutate(
    topic_share = as.double(topic_count) / sum(topic_count, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  # sort on topic_count desc, then call_topic asc to break ties deterministically
  arrange(desc(topic_count), call_topic) |>
  head(10)



Interpretation notes. The top 10 view is useful for quick reporting but may hide important lower-volume issues. In cases where there are not many calls, it may be useful to limit this list to a top 5, or to truncate ties at the end of the list.

title: Additional Notes
text: Field types use Parquet-style names: boolean, date, float, integer, string, time, timestamp. For string columns that behave as factors, the possible values are listed.

title: Additional Notes
text: Field types use Parquet-style names: boolean, date, float, integer, string, time, timestamp. For string columns that behave as factors, the possible values are listed.

title: Additional Notes
text:
Cumulative Fields
Cumulative fields only increase within a window and then reset. They are called out explicitly in each table entry. Never sum cumulative fields across rows; take the latest value for the window you need. See the cookbook Notes for guidance on selecting the right cumulative column for a given reporting window.

title: Additional Notes
text:
Proportions
Proportions are stored as values between 0 and 1, not 0 and 100.

title: About
text:
The data catalog describes the tables available to analysts. It is distinct from the metrics cookbook; the cookbook explains how to derive metrics, while the catalog describes the tables those metrics are calculated from, field by field.


Derived tables in the ibm_verify schema, pulled from the IBM Verify reporting API.

app_login_counts - daily sign-in counts by application

title: Call Centre
text: Weekly call centre data extracted from the CanadaLogin support queue Excel reports. These two tables share the same source and update cadence but are at different grains: weekly_activity_report is one row per week, while weekly_topic_dump is one row per topic record per call. They arrive as two separate files in the same weekly email, so each table has its own extraction pipeline (see the provenance note on each table below).

title: Call Centre
text:
weekly_activity_report
Weekly summary of call centre operations for the CanadaLogin support queue.
Each row represents one reporting week, summarizing call volume, service levels, call outcomes, and call type breakdowns for that week.
Keywords: call centre, calls, weekly, volume, wait time, call length, service level

Provenance
Sourced from a weekly Excel report that is typically sent to the EDCP Data and Research team via email. That spreadsheet is extracted and loaded into the data repository via the call centre weekly activity report Lambda. The extracted_at field records when each row was last extracted from the source file.

Updated: Weekly, typically on Tuesdays
Steward: EDCP Data and Research Team
Contact: Colin Douglas



Fields
One row per reporting week.
Many count fields are stored as float because they originate from Excel. Proportions (fields ending in _pct) are stored as values between 0 and 1, not 0 and 100.








Field Name
Type
Description




week_text
string
Report title, extracted from the Excel file


date_range_start
string
Start date of the reporting week as a text date (for example, “2026-04-26”)


date_range_end
string
End date of the reporting week as a text date (for example, “2026-05-02”)


date
string
Equivalent to date_range_start; used as the primary date key


file
string
Source filename the row was extracted from


extracted_at
string
ISO 8601 timestamp of when the row was extracted from the source file


calls_accepted
float
Total calls accepted by the queue during the week


calls_accepted_pct
float
Proportion of calls accepted (typically 1.0)


calls_abandoned
float
Number of calls abandoned before being answered


calls_abandoned_pct
float
Proportion of accepted calls that were abandoned


calls_answered
float
Number of calls answered by an agent


pct_answered_within_60s
float
Proportion of calls answered within 60 seconds


avg_delay_seconds
float
Average wait time before a call was answered, in seconds


avg_call_length_seconds
float
Average duration of answered calls, in seconds


avg_handle_time_seconds
float
Average total handle time per call (call length plus any wrap-up time), in seconds


calls_completed_by_io
float
Number of calls completed by the information officer


calls_completed_by_io_pct
float
Proportion of answered calls completed by the information officer


hangups
float
Number of calls where the caller hung up


hangups_pct
float
Proportion of answered calls that ended in a hangup


test_calls
float
Number of test calls made during the week


test_calls_pct
float
Proportion of accepted calls that were test calls


total_calls_answered
float
Total calls answered (may differ from calls_answered depending on reporting logic)


total_calls_answered_pct
float
Proportion of accepted calls that were answered


comments
float
Number of calls where a comment was recorded


comments_pct
float
Proportion of answered calls with a comment


callback
float
Number of calls where a callback was requested or offered


callback_pct
float
Proportion of answered calls that involved a callback


no_information_provided
float
Number of calls where no information was provided to the caller


no_information_provided_pct
float
Proportion of answered calls where no information was provided


transfer_to_crisis_centres
float
Number of calls transferred to a crisis centre


transfer_to_crisis_centres_pct
float
Proportion of answered calls transferred to a crisis centre


program_content_provided
float
Number of calls where program content was provided to the caller


program_content_provided_pct
float
Proportion of answered calls where program content was provided


total_of_call_types
float
Total number of call-type records for the week (used as denominator for call-type proportions)


total_of_call_types_pct
float
Proportion of total call types (typically 1.0)





Notes
avg_delay_seconds and avg_call_length_seconds are weekly averages sourced from the report directly. When combining across weeks, use a weighted average with calls_answered as the weight rather than a simple average. See the Call Centre metrics chapter for example SQL.
date_range_start and date_range_end are stored as strings. Cast to DATE in SQL when filtering by date range.

title: Call Centre
text:
weekly_topic_dump
Individual call records from the CanadaLogin support queue, with topic classifications for each call.
Each row represents one topic record associated with a single call. A single call may have multiple rows if multiple topics were recorded.
Keywords: call centre, topics, call reasons, contact drivers, caller issues

Provenance
Sourced from a weekly Excel report that is typically sent to the EDCP Data and Research team via email. That spreadsheet is extracted and loaded into the data repository via the call_centre_etl.py Lambda. The extracted_at field records when each row was last extracted from the source file.

Updated: Weekly, typically on Tuesdays
Steward: EDCP Data and Research Team
Contact: Colin Douglas



Fields
One row per topic record per call. A single call (call_no) may appear on multiple rows if more than one topic was recorded.








Field Name
Type
Description




call_no
integer
Identifier for the call. Not unique - multiple rows share the same call_no when multiple topics were recorded


call_datetime
timestamp
Date and time the call took place


call_date
date
Date the call took place


date
string
Call date as a text date. Mirrors call_date


clienttype
string
Type of caller, for example “Business”, “Public”, “Government of Canada”


language
string
Language of the call, for example “English”, “French”


origin
string
Province or region the caller is calling from


statobject
string
The call topic as recorded in the source system. May contain leading/trailing whitespace or inconsistent spacing


cstat
string
Full topic classification path, including category and topic (for example, “Main Document description::What is CanadaLogin?”)


transactiontype
string
Type of transaction recorded for the topic, for example “Search”, “Callback”


programcontact
string
Program contact information provided to the caller, if any. Often missing


source
float
Unused





Notes
Because a single call can generate multiple topic rows, do not count rows to get call volume. Use COUNT(DISTINCT call_no) or join to weekly_activity_report for call counts.
statobject frequently contains leading/trailing whitespace and repeated internal spaces. Always clean it before grouping:
REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(statobject), '\s+', ' ') AS call_topic
See the Call Centre metrics chapter for topic count and topic share example SQL.
call_datetime is a native timestamp and can be used directly. call_date is the same value truncated to a DATE.

title: IBM Verify
text: Derived tables in the ibm_verify schema, populated from the IBM Verify reporting API. Each table is a daily aggregate derived from the raw event stream in ibm_verify_raw.ibm_verify. The field-level meaning of those raw events is documented separately in the IBM Verify event data dictionary, organized by event type (Authentication Event, MFA Authentication Event, SSO, and so on).

title: IBM Verify
text:
app_login_counts
Daily sign-in counts for each CanadaLogin-integrated application.
Each row represents one application on one day, summarizing that application’s sign-in activity and unique-user reach for the day.
Keywords: sign-ins, logins, applications, unique users, adoption, daily counts

Provenance
Queried directly from the IBM Verify production instance via API.

Updated: Daily, at 6am America/Toronto
Steward: EDCP Data and Research Team
Contact: Yinlin Zhang



Fields
One row per application_name per day.








Field Name
Type
Description




application_name
string
Name of the integrated application (for example, “IRIS - PRC - Prod”)


from_date
timestamp
Start of the reporting day (midnight)


to_date
timestamp
End of the reporting day (23:59:59)


total_logins
integer
Total sign-in events recorded for the application that day. Counts events, not people. Reported as “total sign-ins”


unique_users
integer
Number of distinct users who signed in to the application that day


failed_logins
integer
Number of failed sign-in attempts that day


successful_logins
integer
Number of successful sign-ins that day


mtd_unique_users
integer
Cumulative distinct users for the application month-to-date. Resets at the start of each month


ytd_unique_users
integer
Cumulative distinct users for the application year-to-date. Resets at the start of each calendar year


rolling_365d_unique_users
integer
Distinct users who signed in to this application in the trailing 365 days ending on that row’s date


fetched_at
timestamp
When the aggregation last fetched this row


date
string
The reporting day as a text date (for example, “2026-04-23”). Mirrors from_date





Notes
total_logins, unique_users, failed_logins, and successful_logins are daily values. mtd_unique_users, ytd_unique_users, and rolling_365d_unique_users are cumulative counts of distinct users and should not be summed across rows; take the latest value within the window instead.
rolling_365d_unique_users is null before 2026-06-13, its implementation date.
A since_apr22_unique_users field (cumulative distinct users since the April 22, 2026 public launch) was previously provided here. It has been removed from the pipeline because the source API retains only the most recent 366.5 days of data, so a since-launch cumulative count cannot be maintained once launch is more than a year in the past. See the retention note on the IBM Verify catalog overview.
Unlike auth_total_logins, these counts are not inflated by second factors. The per-authentication-method event inflation described there only happens when a user signs in to CanadaLogin itself. Authenticating against an integrated application reuses the credentials already established in the CanadaLogin session, so the user enters no password or second factor and a single application sign-in records a single event. total_logins and successful_logins still count events rather than people, because one person can sign in to an application repeatedly. Use unique_users when the question is about how many distinct people used the application.

title: IBM Verify
text:
auth_total_logins
Daily, CanadaLogin-wide sign-in totals across all applications.
Each row represents one day and summarizes overall CanadaLogin authentication activity. Unlike app_login_counts, there is no per-application breakdown.
Keywords: sign-ins, logins, totals, CanadaLogin overall, unique users, daily

Provenance
Queried directly from the IBM Verify production instance via API.

Updated: Daily, at 6am America/Toronto
Steward: EDCP Data and Research Team
Contact: Yinlin Zhang



Fields
One row per day.








Field Name
Type
Description




from_date
timestamp
Start of the reporting day (midnight)


to_date
timestamp
End of the reporting day (23:59:59)


unique_users
integer
Number of distinct users who signed in to CanadaLogin that day. In IBM Verify, this is referred to as “unique logins”.


failed_logins
integer
Number of failed sign-in attempts that day


successful_logins
integer
Number of successful authentication events that day.


mtd_unique_users
integer
Cumulative distinct users month-to-date. Resets at the start of each month


ytd_unique_users
integer
Cumulative distinct users year-to-date. Resets at the start of each calendar year


rolling_365d_unique_users
integer
Distinct users who signed in to CanadaLogin in the trailing 365 days ending on that row’s date


last_updated_at
timestamp
When the underlying data for this row was last updated


fetched_at
timestamp
When the aggregation last fetched this row


date
string
The reporting day as a text date (for example, “2026-05-21”). Mirrors from_date





Notes
unique_users, failed_logins, and successful_logins are daily values. The mtd_, ytd_, and rolling_365d_ unique-user fields are cumulative and should not be summed across rows; take the latest value within the window instead.
rolling_365d_unique_users is null before 2026-06-13, its implementation date.
A since_apr22_unique_users field (cumulative distinct users since the April 22, 2026 launch) was previously provided here. It has been removed from the pipeline because the source API retains only the most recent 366.5 days of data, so a since-launch cumulative count cannot be maintained once launch is more than a year in the past. See the retention note on the IBM Verify catalog overview.
When a user signs in to CanadaLogin itself, IBM Verify records each authentication method as a separate event. Entering a username and password followed by a second factor like an OTP counts as two successful events. A mistyped password that is then entered correctly, followed by a correct OTP, counts as three events: two successful and one failed. Both successful_logins and failed_logins are therefore inflated relative to the number of distinct sign-in sessions.
This inflation applies only to signing in to CanadaLogin itself. Authenticating against an integrated application does not inflate these counts, because the user reuses the credentials already established in their CanadaLogin session and enters no password or second factor (see app_login_counts).
Summing successful_logins is therefore a misleading indicator of actual user engagement. Instead, use the unique_users field, which is daily active users, and is a measure of the number of users who engaged with the system in a given day.

title: IBM Verify
text:
mfa_activity
Daily multi-factor authentication (MFA) activity, broken down by factor type and outcome.
Each row represents the count of MFA attempts for a single combination of day, MFA factor type, and result.
Keywords: MFA, multi-factor, second factor, OTP, FIDO2, authentication, security

Provenance
Queried directly from the IBM Verify production instance via API.

Updated: Daily, at 6am America/Toronto
Steward: EDCP Data and Research Team
Contact: Yinlin Zhang



Fields
One row per day per mfa_type per result.








Field Name
Type
Description




from_date
timestamp
Start of the reporting day (midnight)


to_date
timestamp
End of the reporting day (23:59:59)


mfa_type
string
The MFA factor used. One of “sms_otp”, “fido2”, “kq”, “totp”, “voice_otp”, “qr”, “ibm_verify_push”, “email_otp”


result
string
Outcome of the MFA event. One of “success”, “failure”, or “sent”


count
integer
Number of MFA attempts for this day, factor, and result


fetched_at
timestamp
When the aggregation last fetched this row


date
string
The reporting day as a text date (for example, “2026-05-31”). Mirrors from_date





Notes
Only OTP factor types (sms_otp, email_otp, voice_otp) generate a “sent” event, because they are issued before the user responds. Successfully clearing one of these challenges therefore counts as two events: first a “sent” event, then either a “success” or “failure” event depending on how the user responds. It is possible to generate multiple “success”/“failure” events on a single “sent” event if the user inputs their OTP incorrectly first and then correctly. Non-OTP factor types (fido2, kq, totp, ibm_verify_push) have no “sent” event and produce only “success”/“failure” rows.
When calculating MFA usage metrics, de-duplicate accordingly: count “sent” events for OTP factors (sending an email OTP but not using it is still usage), and “success”/“failure” events for non-OTP factors. Summing “sent” alone would silently drop every non-OTP factor.
When calculating success rate metrics, use the number of successful events divided by the sum of the success and failure events, excluding “sent”.

title: Jira (PSOM)
text:
Tables in the jira schema, populated by the weekly PSOM Jira Service Management sync pipeline.
Each Monday at ~6:00 AM ET, the psom-weekly-sync Lambda fetches a full snapshot of all tickets from the PSOM project (jtickets.atlassian.net) and writes it as a Parquet partition in S3. The Glue crawler then updates the Athena table schema.

title: Jira (PSOM)
text:
PSOM
Weekly snapshot of all PSOM Jira Service Management tickets.
One row per Jira issue per weekly snapshot. Each Monday run writes a full snapshot of all current PSOM tickets to a new date= partition. Previous partitions are preserved as an audit trail, enabling point-in-time analysis.
Jira source: jtickets.atlassian.net, project PSOM, fetched via POST /rest/api/3/search/jql with full comment history via /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment.
Keywords: jira, psom, tickets, service requests, SLA, support

Provenance
Queried from PSOM Jira Cloud via API by the psom-weekly-sync Lambda function.

Updated: Weekly, every Monday at ~06:00 ET
Steward: EDCP Data and Research Team
Contact: Yinlin Zhang (Data and Research team)



Fields
One row per Jira issue per weekly snapshot (date partition).








Field Name
Type
Description




date
varchar
Partition key: the date the Lambda ran (YYYY-MM-DD).


key
varchar
Jira ticket key, e.g. PSOM-42.


summary
varchar
One-line ticket title.


description
varchar
Full ticket description, extracted from Atlassian Document Format (ADF) to plain text.


issue_type
varchar
Jira issue type name, e.g. Email Request, Partner Support Request form.


status
varchar
Current workflow status, e.g. Done, Canceled, Escalated to PTT, Escalated to Auth PT, Ready to archive.


status_changed
varchar
ISO 8601 timestamp when the ticket’s status category last changed.


priority
varchar
Priority level, e.g. High, Normal, Low, Critical.


assignee
varchar
Display name of the current assignee; empty string if unassigned.


reporter
varchar
Display name of the person who reported the ticket.


creator
varchar
Display name of the person who created the ticket (may differ from reporter).


created
varchar
ISO 8601 timestamp when the ticket was created.


updated
varchar
ISO 8601 timestamp of the most recent field or comment update.


due_date
varchar
Due date in YYYY-MM-DD format; empty string if not set.


labels
varchar
Semicolon-separated list of labels applied to the ticket; empty string if none.


request_type
varchar
JSM service request type name, e.g. New Access Request. Empty for tickets created internally through Jira rather than the customer-facing portal (all PSOM tickets so far), which never set a request type.


request_participants
varchar
Semicolon-separated display names of request participants (CC’d users).


organizations
varchar
Semicolon-separated names of JSM organizations the request is associated with.


sla_first_response_due
varchar
ISO 8601 datetime the “Time to first response” SLA must be (or was) met by, per the SLA goal. For an open ticket this is the upcoming breach deadline; for a closed cycle, when the cycle stopped. Empty if no SLA clock.


sla_first_response_breached
varchar
"True" if the first-response SLA was missed (no agent response within the agreed 4-hour window), "False" if it was met or is still within the window, "" if no SLA clock. Stored as a string, not a native boolean.


sla_time_to_done_due
varchar
ISO 8601 datetime the “Time to done” SLA must be (or was) met by, per the SLA goal. For an open ticket this is the upcoming breach deadline; for a closed cycle, when the cycle stopped. Empty if no SLA clock.


sla_time_to_done_breached
varchar
"True" if the resolution SLA was missed (ticket not resolved within the agreed 16-hour window), "False" if it was met or is still within the window, "" if no SLA clock. Stored as a string, not a native boolean.


comment_count
bigint
Total number of comments on the ticket.


comments
varchar
JSON array string of all comments. Each element: {"author": "...", "created": "...", "updated": "...", "body": "...", "internal": false}. The body is ADF-extracted plain text. The internal flag is true for JSM internal notes.


fetched_at
varchar
UTC timestamp when the Lambda wrote this row (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS).





Notes
All timestamp columns (created, updated, status_changed, sla_*_due, fetched_at) are stored as varchar.
SLA fields (sla_first_response_*, sla_time_to_done_*) are populated for tickets with an active JSM SLA clock. The *_breached columns hold the strings "True"/"False", not a native boolean, so compare against 'True'/'False' (not a bare boolean) and cast if you need one. "False" covers both SLAs that were met and open tickets still inside their window, so a breach rate computed on a single snapshot is a lower bound: an open ticket counted as "False" today can still breach later. Snapshots up to and including date=2026-07-06 predate the SLA pipeline fix and have these columns empty; from date=2026-07-07 on they are populated. Exclude empty-string rows when calculating breach rates.
Each weekly run adds a new date= partition. All previous partitions are retained, so you can compare snapshots across weeks to track status changes over time.

title: Platform
text: Platform metrics for CanadaLogin, in the sre schema. Currently, both tables are derived from the CanadaLogin Platform team’s CanadaLogin_MTTR_Dashboard Google Sheet (maintained by hand in the CDS Google Drive) and fetched by the sre-mttr-sheets-fetcher Lambda using Google Sheets API 6am ET every Monday. They share one source and grain (one row per incident); they differ only in history: mttr_current holds the latest state of each incident, while mttr_history accumulates a dated snapshot of every incident on each upload.

title: Platform
text:
mttr_current
The latest known state of every CanadaLogin incident, one row per incident.
Each row is a single incident. The table is overwritten on each upload with the most recent values, so it never grows beyond the current incident set. For point-in-time history, see mttr_history.
Keywords: MTTR, MTTA, MTTM, incident, incident response, postmortem, severity, reliability, SRE, mean time to resolve

Provenance
Fetched automatically every Monday at 06:00 ET from the CanadaLogin MTTR Dashboard Google Sheet using the sre-mttr-sheets-fetcher Lambda. Each upload overwrites this table wholesale with the current incident state and appends a dated snapshot to mttr_history.

Updated: Weekly, Every Monday 06:00 ET
Steward: EDCP Data and Research Team
Contact: Yinlin Zhang (Data and Research team) or Obie Okongwu (CL Platform Team)



Fields
One row per incident, keyed by incident_id. See the schema-level Data quality note for caveats that apply to the string timestamps, URL columns, free-text categories, and rounded durations.








Field Name
Type
Description




incident_id
string
Unique identifier for the incident, format INC-NNN


slack_channel
string
URL of the incident’s dedicated Slack channel


severity
string
Incident severity. One of “Sev1”, “Sev2”, “Sev3”, or “Sev4” (Sev1 most severe, Sev4 least). Free text in the source, not strictly enforced


service
string
The CanadaLogin service affected


component
string
The specific subsystem affected, more granular than service. Free text


short_description
string
One-line free-text summary of the incident


detected_at
string
When the incident was detected, America/Toronto local time at minute precision (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM)


acknowledged_at
string
When the incident was acknowledged. Same format and timezone as detected_at


mitigated_at
string
When the incident was mitigated. Same format and timezone as detected_at


resolved_at
string
When the incident was fully resolved. Same format and timezone as detected_at


root_cause_category
string
Free-text root-cause classification


mtta_alert_ack
integer
Minutes from detected_at to acknowledged_at, rounded to the nearest minute


mttm_detected_mitigated
integer
Minutes from detected_at to mitigated_at, rounded to the nearest minute


mttr_detected_resolved
integer
Minutes from detected_at to resolved_at, rounded to the nearest minute


postmortem_link
string
URL of the incident’s postmortem Google Doc. Optional


week
integer
ISO-8601 week number (1-53) of detected_at


month
string
Calendar month of detected_at, first-of-month formatted as mmm-yy text (for example “Feb-26”)


fetched_at
timestamp
When the pipeline ingested this snapshot, in UTC

title: Platform
text:
mttr_history
The append-only companion to mttr_current: same source, columns, and provenance, but instead of holding only the latest state it accumulates a dated snapshot of every incident on each upload.
Each row is one incident (incident_id) as it appeared on one snapshot date, so an incident’s metrics can change from one snapshot to the next as it is re-triaged. A row is unique on (incident_id, date).
Keywords: MTTR, MTTA, MTTM, incident, incident response, snapshot, history, severity, reliability, SRE, mean time to resolve

Fields
Every column is identical to mttr_current, with one addition:








Field Name
Type
Description




date
string
Snapshot date as YYYY-MM-DD text; the grain that lets this table accumulate. Equals the date portion of fetched_at

title: Platform
text:
Uptime Tables
The uptime tables are sourced from the cds-snc/status-statut public GitHub repository and updated automatically each day. No manual steps required.

title: Platform
text:
uptime_summary
One row per monitored site per day. Each row is a snapshot of rolling-window statistics fetched from history/summary.json in the cds-snc/status-statut GitHub repository at 04:00 ET.

Provenance
Fully automated. No manual upload required. The upptime summary.yml GitHub Action recalculates all rolling-window metrics from the full five-minute ping history once per day (~00:00 UTC), rewriting summary.json. The Lambda runs at 04:00 ET and fetches that file, writing one Parquet row per site into the date=YYYY-MM-DD partition corresponding to the preceding day.

Updated: Daily, at ~04:00 ET
Steward: EDCP Data and Research Team
Contact: Yinlin Zhang (Data and Research team)

Six monitored sites:








slug
lang
url




canada-login-website-en
WEB-EN
https://login.canada.ca


canada-login-website-fr
WEB-FR
https://connexion.canada.ca


canada-login-manage-profile-staging
MP-STAGING
https://app.login-connexion.alpha.canada.ca


canada-login-manage-profile-prod
MP-PROD
https://app.login-connexion.canada.ca


canada-login-idp-staging
IDP-STAGING
https://auth.login-connexion.alpha.canada.ca


canada-login-idp-prod
IDP-PROD
https://auth.login-connexion.canada.ca





Fields








Field Name
Type
Description




date
varchar
Hive partition key: the date the Lambda ran for (yesterday’s date, YYYY-MM-DD).


fetch_date
varchar
UTC timestamp when the Lambda fetched the data (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS). Useful for diagnosing staleness.


slug
varchar
Upptime site identifier. One of the six values in the table above.


lang
varchar
Short label identifying the site variant, one of the six values in the table above.


url
varchar
The monitored URL.


status
varchar
Site status at fetch time: up or down.


uptime_day
double
Uptime percentage over the past 24 hours (~288 five-minute pings).


uptime_week
double
Uptime percentage over the past 7 days (~2,016 pings).


uptime_month
double
Uptime percentage over the past 30 days (~8,640 pings).


uptime_year
double
Uptime percentage over the past 365 days (~105,120 pings).


uptime_alltime
double
Uptime percentage across all pings since monitoring began (April 2, 2026).


response_day_ms
bigint
Average response time over the past 24 hours, in milliseconds.


response_week_ms
bigint
Average response time over the past 7 days, in milliseconds.


response_month_ms
bigint
Average response time over the past 30 days, in milliseconds.


response_year_ms
bigint
Average response time over the past 365 days, in milliseconds.


response_alltime_ms
bigint
Average response time across all pings since monitoring began, in milliseconds.


daily_minutes_down
varchar
JSON string mapping each outage date to total minutes down on that date.





Notes
Timing note. When the Lambda runs on June 30, it fetches rolling-window statistics last recalculated at ~23:2x UTC on June 29. The resulting row lands in partition date=2026-06-29. All rolling windows trail backward from that reference point.

title: Platform
text:
uptime_timeseries
One row per upptime bot commit per site. The upptime bot commits to the cds-snc/status-statut history YAML files once per day (~23:2x UTC) as a daily snapshot, plus additional commits immediately when a status change is detected mid-day.

Provenance
Fully automated. No manual upload required. Sourced from the GitHub Commits API for each site’s history file (history/<slug>.yml).

Updated: Daily, at ~04:00 ET
Steward: EDCP Data and Research Team
Contact: Yinlin Zhang (Data and Research team)

Expected row count per site per day:



Scenario
Expected rows




Normal all-up day
1 (daily ~23:2x UTC snapshot, status = 'up')


Outage day
2+ (down event + recovery event + daily snapshot)





Fields








Field Name
Type
Description




date
varchar
Hive partition key: the UTC date of the commit (YYYY-MM-DD). Use in WHERE date = '...' or WHERE CAST(date AS DATE) BETWEEN ... filters.


ping_timestamp
varchar
UTC timestamp of the upptime bot’s commit (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS). On a normal day this is the daily snapshot at ~23:2x UTC. On an outage day earlier commits record when the site went down or recovered.


slug
varchar
Upptime site identifier, same six values as in sre.uptime_summary.


lang
varchar
Short label, same six values as in sre.uptime_summary.


status
varchar
Ping result at the moment of this commit: up, down, or unknown.


http_code
bigint
HTTP status code at the moment of this commit. 200 on a normal day; 0 or 503 when down. null if unparseable.


response_ms
bigint
Response time in milliseconds at the moment of this commit. Single-ping measurement at commit time, not a daily average. null if unparseable.





Notes
response_ms is a single-ping measurement. It records the response time at the exact moment of the upptime bot’s commit, not a daily average. For averaged response times use sre.uptime_summary (response_day_ms, response_week_ms, etc.), which are computed from all 288 five-minute pings.
status = 'down' rows count detection events, not outage duration. Each down row is one commit, one moment when upptime detected a failure. For total minutes of downtime on a given date, use the daily_minutes_down JSON column in sre.uptime_summary.

title: IBM Verify
text:
Metrics in this section are calculated from IBM Verify authentication and application sign-in data.
The relevant IBM Verify tables are:
Internal applications. The following applications are typically excluded from public-facing adoption metrics:

title: IBM Verify
text:
CanadaLogin Metrics
Metrics in this section describe CanadaLogin overall, across all applications, sourced mainly from ibm_verify.auth_total_logins (the estimated sessions metric also draws on ibm_verify.mfa_activity).

Sign-Ins
The total number of sign-in events to CanadaLogin, including both successful and failed attempts.
Alternative names:

total sign-in attempts
sign-ins to CanadaLogin in a reporting period
total sign-ins to CanadaLogin

Calculation. Sum failed_logins and successful_logins over the selected reporting period.
To track volume over time, bin the same sum by week. Truncating from_date to the week groups each day into its Monday-anchored bin, so the result is one sign-in total per Monday-to-Sunday week.

SQLR


Overall:
SELECT
    SUM(failed_logins + successful_logins) AS total_sign_in_attempts
FROM ibm_verify.auth_total_logins
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28';
By week:
SELECT
    date_trunc('week', from_date) AS week_start,
    SUM(failed_logins + successful_logins) AS total_sign_in_attempts
FROM ibm_verify.auth_total_logins
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
-- date_trunc('week', ...) anchors each bin to its Monday
GROUP BY date_trunc('week', from_date)
ORDER BY week_start;


Overall:
tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "auth_total_logins")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    total_sign_in_attempts = sum(failed_logins + successful_logins, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
By week:
tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "auth_total_logins")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28")
  ) |>
  # floor_date(..., "week") translates to Athena's date_trunc('week', ...),
  # which anchors each bin to its Monday
  mutate(week_start = floor_date(from_date, "week")) |>
  group_by(week_start) |>
  summarise(
    total_sign_in_attempts = sum(failed_logins + successful_logins, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(week_start)



Interpretation notes. This counts authentication events, not people. A single sign-in that uses a second factor records more than one successful event (see the auth_total_logins caveat), so the total is inflated relative to distinct user activity. Use a unique-user metric when the question is about reach. In the weekly view, the first and last weeks of the reporting window are usually partial (the window rarely starts or ends on a Monday), so a low count in a boundary week may reflect fewer days rather than a real dip in volume.


Sign-In Success Rate
The proportion of sign-in attempts that succeeded, across all of CanadaLogin.
This metric is useful for monitoring overall authentication health. A sustained drop can indicate a service problem, a misconfigured application, or elevated attack traffic inflating the failure count.
Alternative names:

authentication success rate
login success rate
overall sign-in success rate
successful sign-in proportion

Calculation. Divide the sum of successful_logins by the sum of all attempts (successful_logins + failed_logins) over the selected reporting period.
To track the metric over time, bin the same calculation by week. Truncating from_date to the week groups each day into its Monday-anchored bin, so the result is one success rate per Monday-to-Sunday week.

SQLR


Overall:
SELECT
    CAST(SUM(successful_logins) AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(SUM(successful_logins + failed_logins), 0) AS sign_in_success_rate
FROM ibm_verify.auth_total_logins
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28';
By week:
SELECT
    date_trunc('week', from_date) AS week_start,
    CAST(SUM(successful_logins) AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(SUM(successful_logins + failed_logins), 0) AS sign_in_success_rate
FROM ibm_verify.auth_total_logins
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
-- date_trunc('week', ...) anchors each bin to its Monday
GROUP BY date_trunc('week', from_date)
ORDER BY week_start;


Overall:
tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "auth_total_logins")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    sign_in_success_rate =
      sum(as.double(successful_logins), na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(successful_logins + failed_logins, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
By week:
tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "auth_total_logins")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28")
  ) |>
  # floor_date(..., "week") translates to Athena's date_trunc('week', ...),
  # which anchors each bin to its Monday
  mutate(week_start = floor_date(from_date, "week")) |>
  group_by(week_start) |>
  summarise(
    sign_in_success_rate =
      sum(as.double(successful_logins), na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(successful_logins + failed_logins, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(week_start)



Interpretation notes. The rate is a proportion between 0 and 1. Read it alongside sign-in volume: a low success rate on very low volume may just be noise, while the same rate on high volume is a real signal. In the weekly view, the first and last weeks of the reporting window are usually partial (the window rarely starts or ends on a Monday), so treat boundary weeks with caution when comparing trends.
This rate is computed over authentication methods, not sessions. When signing in to CanadaLogin, IBM Verify records each method as its own event, so a mistyped password is a failed event even when the user then succeeds and completes the sign-in (see the auth_total_logins caveat). The figure measures how often individual authentication steps succeed, not how often a sign-in session ultimately completes. This differs from the per-application rate, which measures credential validity against a relying party with no factors entered.


Completed Sign-In Sessions (Estimated)
An estimate of the number of completed sign-in sessions to CanadaLogin over a selected reporting period, where a session is one user getting all the way through the sign-in flow regardless of how many authentication methods it took.
This is an estimate, not an exact count. The documented tables hold daily counts of authentication method events with no session identifier, so true sessions cannot be reconstructed from them (that would require the correlationid on the raw event stream, which is not in the data catalog). The figure below is a defensible approximation built from two tables, and it carries the assumptions listed under Interpretation notes. Read those before using it.
Alternative names:

estimated sign-in sessions
completed logins
estimated login count
sign-in sessions

Calculation. A completed session has exactly one successful first-factor event (the password) plus zero or more successful second factors. Both kinds of success are lumped together in successful_logins on auth_total_logins, while the second-factor successes are counted separately on mfa_activity. Subtracting the second from the first leaves one event per session: the first-factor success. So sum successful_logins over the window and subtract the sum of mfa_activity.count where result = 'success'.

SQLR


WITH first_factor AS (
    SELECT SUM(successful_logins) AS successful_events
    FROM ibm_verify.auth_total_logins
    -- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
    WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
),

second_factor AS (
    SELECT SUM(count) AS mfa_successes
    FROM ibm_verify.mfa_activity
    -- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
    WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
      -- outcome events only; a "sent" OTP is an issuance, not a success
      AND result = 'success'
)

SELECT
    first_factor.successful_events - second_factor.mfa_successes
        AS estimated_completed_sessions
FROM first_factor
CROSS JOIN second_factor;


successful_events <- tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "auth_total_logins")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28")
  ) |>
  summarise(successful_events = sum(as.double(successful_logins), na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  pull(successful_events)

mfa_successes <- tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "mfa_activity")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28"),
    # outcome events only; a "sent" OTP is an issuance, not a success
    result == "success"
  ) |>
  summarise(mfa_successes = sum(as.double(count), na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  pull(mfa_successes)

successful_events - mfa_successes



Interpretation notes. The estimate works because each session contributes exactly one first-factor success no matter how many second factors it used:









Session type
successful_logins
MFA successes
Net (= sessions)




Password only
1
0
1


Password + OTP
2
1
1


Password + step-up (2 factors)
3
2
1


Mistyped password, then OTP
2 (the failure lands in failed_logins)
1
1



This holds only because the method-event inflation applies to signing in to CanadaLogin itself (see the auth_total_logins caveat). It does not apply to authenticating against an integrated application, so this metric describes CanadaLogin sign-in sessions, not application sign-ins.
The estimate rests on two assumptions:

Passwordless and FIDO-first logins are undercounted. A session whose only factor is FIDO2 is one event in successful_logins and also a fido2 success in mfa_activity, so it nets to zero. The figure is therefore a slight underestimate. This has been tolerable only because All MFA Activity by Factor shows fido2 historically sees little use; with passkeys rolled out to the public on 2026-07-06, passwordless adoption is expected to grow, and this undercount grows with it.
The two tables are assumed to count second-factor successes the same way. Both derive from the same IBM Verify event stream, which makes this plausible, but it is not guaranteed by anything in the data catalog.

Because of these assumptions, label the result as estimated wherever it is reported, and never present it as an exact session count. When the question is about distinct people rather than sessions, prefer unique_users (daily active users), which needs no assumptions: a user cannot be a unique user without completing a sign-in.


Cumulative Unique Users
The total number of distinct users who have signed in to CanadaLogin over a chosen reporting window: this month or this calendar year.
This metric is useful for understanding the overall reach and adoption of CanadaLogin over time. Note that it will not align with the sum of unique users across individual apps: a user who signs into App A and App B on the same day counts as two unique users at the app level but only one overall.
Alternative names:

month-to-date unique users
year-to-date unique users
total overall unique users this month / this year
overall unique users MTD / YTD
distinct people reached on CanadaLogin

Calculation. Take the latest value of the cumulative unique-user column for the window of interest. The window is chosen by which column you read, not by a date filter (see the note on reporting timespans):

mtd_unique_users for month-to-date
ytd_unique_users for year-to-date

These columns are cumulative, so the most recent row holds the current total.
A since_apr22_unique_users column (cumulative distinct users since launch) was previously available but has been removed; see the data retention note.

SQLR


Month-to-date:
SELECT
    mtd_unique_users
FROM ibm_verify.auth_total_logins
ORDER BY from_date DESC
LIMIT 1;
Year-to-date:
SELECT
    ytd_unique_users
FROM ibm_verify.auth_total_logins
ORDER BY from_date DESC
LIMIT 1;


Month-to-date:
tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "auth_total_logins")) |>
  slice_max(
    from_date,
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  pull(mtd_unique_users)
Year-to-date:
tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "auth_total_logins")) |>
  slice_max(
    from_date,
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  pull(ytd_unique_users)



Interpretation notes. Each column resets on its own cadence: mtd_unique_users resets at the start of each month and ytd_unique_users at the start of each calendar year. Do not sum these columns across rows; they are already cumulative.

title: IBM Verify
text:
Application Metrics
Metrics in this section describe sign-in activity broken down by application, sourced from ibm_verify.app_login_counts.

Sign-Ins by Application
The total number of sign-ins recorded for each application over a selected time period.
This metric is useful for understanding overall usage volume across CanadaLogin-integrated applications. It counts sign-in events, not people. A single user may sign in multiple times and contribute multiple sign-ins.
This metric is referred to in the source data as total_logins, but in reporting language it is usually clearer to describe it as total sign-ins.
Alternative names:

total sign-ins
sign-in volume
number of sign-ins
application sign-in count
total uses of CanadaLogin by application

Calculation. Sum total_logins over the selected reporting period, grouped by application.

SQLR


SELECT
  application_name,
  SUM(total_logins) AS total_sign_ins
FROM   ibm_verify.app_login_counts
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE  from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
-- optionally filter out internal applications
  AND application_name NOT IN (
      'Flow Application',
      'GCS Migration Solution',
      'GC Sign In - Profile Management App'
  )
GROUP  BY application_name
ORDER  BY total_sign_ins DESC;


internal_apps <- c(
  "Flow Application",
  "GCS Migration Solution",
  "GC Sign In - Profile Management App"
)

tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "app_login_counts")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28"),
    # optionally filter out internal applications
    !application_name %in% internal_apps
  ) |>
  group_by(application_name) |>
  summarise(
    total_sign_ins = sum(total_logins, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(total_sign_ins))



Interpretation notes. A sign-in count measures events, not people. Use this metric when the question is about system usage or activity volume. Use unique user metrics when the question is about reach.
Unlike the CanadaLogin-wide sign-in count, these per-application counts are not inflated by second factors. Authenticating against an application reuses the credentials from the CanadaLogin session, so each application sign-in is a single event rather than one event per authentication method.


Sign-In Success Rate by Application
The proportion of sign-in attempts that succeeded, broken down by application.
This metric helps isolate authentication problems to a specific application. An application with a markedly lower success rate than its peers is worth investigating for integration or configuration issues.
Alternative names:

authentication success rate by application
login success rate by application
per-application sign-in success rate

Calculation. Divide the sum of successful_logins by the sum of all attempts (successful_logins + failed_logins) over the selected reporting period, grouped by application.

SQLR


SELECT
    application_name,
    CAST(SUM(successful_logins) AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(SUM(successful_logins + failed_logins), 0) AS sign_in_success_rate
FROM ibm_verify.app_login_counts
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
-- optionally filter out internal applications
  AND application_name NOT IN (
      'Flow Application',
      'GCS Migration Solution',
      'GC Sign In - Profile Management App'
  )
GROUP BY application_name
ORDER BY sign_in_success_rate DESC;


internal_apps <- c(
  "Flow Application",
  "GCS Migration Solution",
  "GC Sign In - Profile Management App"
)

tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "app_login_counts")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28"),
    # optionally filter out internal applications
    !application_name %in% internal_apps
  ) |>
  group_by(application_name) |>
  summarise(
    sign_in_success_rate =
      sum(as.double(successful_logins), na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(successful_logins + failed_logins, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(sign_in_success_rate))



Interpretation notes. Compare applications against each other rather than against an absolute target. Note that this measures authentication against a relying party after already signing in to CanadaLogin. It does not require entering a password or a second factor; it simply checks whether a given credential is valid for signing in to an app.


Peak Daily Unique Users
The highest number of unique users observed on any single day during the reporting period.
This metric is useful for identifying the maximum daily reach of an application and for understanding peak demand.
Alternative names:

peak daily users
highest daily unique users
maximum daily active users
busiest day by unique users
peak daily reach

Calculation. Take the maximum value of unique_users over the selected reporting period, grouped by application.

SQLR


WITH ranked AS (
    SELECT
        application_name,
        from_date,
        unique_users,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY application_name
            ORDER BY unique_users DESC, from_date ASC
        ) AS rn
    FROM ibm_verify.app_login_counts
    -- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
    WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
    -- to exclude internal applications from adoption reporting, uncomment:
    -- AND application_name NOT IN (
    --     'Flow Application',
    --     'GCS Migration Solution',
    --     'GC Sign In - Profile Management App'
    -- )
)

SELECT
    application_name,
    from_date,
    unique_users AS peak_daily_unique_users
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY peak_daily_unique_users DESC;


tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "app_login_counts")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28")
    # to exclude internal applications from adoption reporting, add as a filter:
    # !application_name %in% c(
    #   "Flow Application",
    #   "GCS Migration Solution",
    #   "GC Sign In - Profile Management App"
    # )
  ) |>
  group_by(application_name) |>
  slice_max(
    unique_users,
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  select(
    application_name,
    from_date,
    peak_daily_unique_users = unique_users
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(peak_daily_unique_users))



Interpretation notes. This metric identifies the busiest single day by unique user reach. The query above also returns the date on which that peak occurred.


Cumulative Unique Users by Application
The number of distinct users observed for each application over a chosen reporting window: this month or this calendar year.
The cumulative unique-user columns increase or remain stable over time. This metric is closer to “how many distinct people have used the application in this window” than the daily unique user metrics.
Alternative names:

month-to-date unique users by application
year-to-date unique users by application
distinct user reach per application
total unique people reached per application

Calculation. Take the latest value of the cumulative unique-user column over the selected window, grouped by application. The window is chosen by which column you read (see the note on reporting timespans):

mtd_unique_users for month-to-date
ytd_unique_users for year-to-date

The WHERE from_date BETWEEN ... bounds set the “as of” reporting date; the most recent row in the window holds the current cumulative total.
A since_apr22_unique_users column (cumulative distinct users since launch) was previously available but has been removed; see the data retention note.

SQLR


Month-to-date:
WITH ranked AS (
    SELECT
        application_name,
        from_date,
        mtd_unique_users,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY application_name
            ORDER BY from_date DESC
        ) AS rn
    FROM ibm_verify.app_login_counts
    WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-06-01' AND DATE '2026-06-08'
    -- to exclude internal applications from adoption reporting, uncomment:
    -- AND application_name NOT IN (
    --     'Flow Application',
    --     'GCS Migration Solution',
    --     'GC Sign In - Profile Management App'
    -- )
)

SELECT
    application_name,
    mtd_unique_users
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY mtd_unique_users DESC;
Year-to-date:
WITH ranked AS (
    SELECT
        application_name,
        from_date,
        ytd_unique_users,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY application_name
            ORDER BY from_date DESC
        ) AS rn
    FROM ibm_verify.app_login_counts
    WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-06-08'
    -- to exclude internal applications from adoption reporting, uncomment:
    -- AND application_name NOT IN (
    --     'Flow Application',
    --     'GCS Migration Solution',
    --     'GC Sign In - Profile Management App'
    -- )
)

SELECT
    application_name,
    ytd_unique_users
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY ytd_unique_users DESC;


Month-to-date:
tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "app_login_counts")) |>
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-06-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-06-08")
    # to exclude internal applications from adoption reporting, add as a filter:
    # !application_name %in% c(
    #   "Flow Application",
    #   "GCS Migration Solution",
