    #   "GC Sign In - Profile Management App"
    # )
  ) |>
  group_by(application_name) |>
  slice_max(
    from_date,
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  select(
    application_name,
    mtd_unique_users
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(mtd_unique_users))
Year-to-date:
tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "app_login_counts")) |>
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-06-08")
    # to exclude internal applications from adoption reporting, add as a filter:
    # !application_name %in% c(
    #   "Flow Application",
    #   "GCS Migration Solution",
    #   "GC Sign In - Profile Management App"
    # )
  ) |>
  group_by(application_name) |>
  slice_max(
    from_date,
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  select(
    application_name,
    ytd_unique_users
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(ytd_unique_users))



Interpretation notes. Each column resets on its own cadence: mtd_unique_users resets at the start of each month and ytd_unique_users at the start of each calendar year. Do not sum these columns across rows; they are already cumulative.


Applications per Credential
The average number of distinct applications a single credential signs in to.
This metric measures how far the typical user spreads across CanadaLogin-integrated applications. It works because per-application unique_users counts each person once per application, while the CanadaLogin-wide count counts each person once overall: a user active on three applications adds three to the per-application sum but only one to the overall total. Dividing the two recovers the average number of applications per credential.
A value of 1.0 means the typical credential touches exactly one application; 2.0 means two, and so on.
Alternative names:

average applications per user
application breadth per credential
multi-app usage
cross-application reach
apps per account

Calculation. Sum the latest year-to-date unique users across every application (from app_login_counts), then divide by the latest CanadaLogin-wide year-to-date unique users (from auth_total_logins). Both inputs are cumulative ytd_unique_users fields, so take the latest value per source rather than summing across days.

SQLR


WITH app_latest AS (
    SELECT
        application_name,
        ytd_unique_users,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY application_name
            ORDER BY from_date DESC
        ) AS rn
    FROM ibm_verify.app_login_counts
),

app_total AS (
    SELECT SUM(ytd_unique_users) AS sum_app_unique_users
    FROM app_latest
    WHERE rn = 1
),

overall_total AS (
    SELECT ytd_unique_users AS overall_unique_users
    FROM ibm_verify.auth_total_logins
    ORDER BY from_date DESC
    LIMIT 1
)

SELECT
    CAST(app_total.sum_app_unique_users AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(overall_total.overall_unique_users, 0) AS applications_per_credential
FROM app_total
CROSS JOIN overall_total;


sum_app_unique_users <- tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "app_login_counts")) |>
  group_by(application_name) |>
  slice_max(
    from_date,
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  summarise(
    sum_app_unique_users = sum(as.double(ytd_unique_users), na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  pull(sum_app_unique_users)

overall_unique_users <- tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "auth_total_logins")) |>
  slice_max(
    from_date,
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  pull(ytd_unique_users)

sum_app_unique_users / overall_unique_users



Interpretation notes. This is a population average, not a per-user count: a small group of power users spanning many applications can pull it up while most credentials stay on one.
The figure is inflated by internal applications, especially the migration app (GCS Migration Solution) and the profile management app (GC Sign In - Profile Management App). A user signing in to a single public service is still recorded as a unique user in the migration app when their credential is migrated from ECM, and in the profile management app when they manage their account, so they count two or three times in the per-application sum while counting once overall. To measure breadth across public-facing services, exclude the internal applications (listed at the top of this chapter) from the per-application sum.
Swap ytd_unique_users for mtd_unique_users to measure the same breadth over a different window.


Average Daily Sign-Ins
The average number of sign-ins per day for each application over a selected reporting period.
This metric summarizes daily sign-in activity into a single per-application average value. It is useful for understanding typical daily usage during a launch period, reporting window, or monitoring period.
Alternative names:

average sign-ins per day
typical daily sign-in volume
mean daily login volume
average daily sign-ins per application

Calculation. For each application, calculate the average of total_logins across all daily records in the selected reporting period.

SQLR


SELECT
    application_name,
    AVG(total_logins) AS average_daily_sign_ins
FROM ibm_verify.app_login_counts
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-04-22' AND DATE '2026-05-31'
-- to exclude internal applications from adoption reporting, uncomment:
-- AND application_name NOT IN (
--     'Flow Application',
--     'GCS Migration Solution',
--     'GC Sign In - Profile Management App'
-- )
GROUP BY application_name
ORDER BY average_daily_sign_ins DESC;


tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "app_login_counts")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-04-22"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
    # to exclude internal applications from adoption reporting, add as a filter:
    # !application_name %in% c(
    #   "Flow Application",
    #   "GCS Migration Solution",
    #   "GC Sign In - Profile Management App"
    # )
  ) |>
  group_by(application_name) |>
  summarise(
    average_daily_sign_ins = mean(total_logins, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(average_daily_sign_ins))



Interpretation notes. The average is taken over the daily rows present for each application in the window, not over every calendar day. If an application has no row on a day with zero activity, that day does not pull the average down, so the figure reflects typical activity on active days.

title: IBM Verify
text:
Multi-factor Authentication Metrics
Metrics in this section describe multi-factor authentication (MFA) activity, sourced from ibm_verify.mfa_activity. That table has one row per day per MFA factor (mfa_type) per outcome (result), with the number of attempts in count.

MFA Authentications
The total number of MFA attempts over a selected reporting period.
This metric measures second-factor authentication volume. It counts attempts, not people, and includes both successful and failed challenges.
Alternative names:

MFA attempts
two-factor authentications
total MFA challenges
MFA volume

Calculation. Sum count over the selected reporting period. OTP factor types (sms_otp, email_otp, voice_otp) record both a sent event and a subsequent success/failure event for the same challenge; to avoid double-counting, only the sent row is used for these types. Non-OTP factor types (fido2, kq, totp, ibm_verify_push) do not generate a sent event, so their success/failure rows are used instead. Add a result breakdown to separate successes from failures.

SQLR


SELECT
    SUM(count) AS mfa_authentications
FROM ibm_verify.mfa_activity
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
  -- OTP types record both a "sent" event and a "success"/"failure" event for
  -- the same challenge. Counting all three would double-count these attempts.
  -- Non-OTP types (fido2, kq, totp, ibm_verify_push) have no "sent" event
  -- and must be counted through their "success"/"failure" rows instead.
  AND (
    result = 'sent'
    OR (
      result IN ('success', 'failure')
      AND mfa_type NOT IN ('sms_otp', 'email_otp', 'voice_otp')
    )
  );


otp_types <- c("sms_otp", "email_otp", "voice_otp")

tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "mfa_activity")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28"),
    # OTP types record both a "sent" and a "success"/"failure" event per
    # challenge; use only "sent" for these to avoid double-counting.
    # Non-OTP types have no "sent" event, so use "success"/"failure".
    result == "sent" |
      (result %in% c("success", "failure") & !mfa_type %in% otp_types)
  ) |>
  summarise(
    mfa_authentications = sum(count, na.rm = TRUE)
  )





MFA Success Rate
The proportion of MFA attempts that succeeded over a selected reporting period.
This metric is useful for monitoring how often users clear the second factor. A falling rate can point to user friction with a particular factor, delivery problems (for example, undelivered SMS or email OTPs), or attack traffic.
Alternative names:

MFA pass rate
second-factor success rate
MFA completion rate

Calculation. Divide the sum of count where result = 'success' by the sum of count where result is 'success' or 'failure'. OTP factor types also produce a 'sent' event per challenge, which must be excluded from both the numerator and the denominator, because sent rows do not represent outcomes.
To track the metric over time, bin the same calculation by week. Truncating from_date to the week groups each day into its Monday-anchored bin, so the result is one success rate per Monday-to-Sunday week.

SQLR


Overall:
SELECT
    CAST(SUM(count) FILTER (WHERE result = 'success') AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(SUM(count), 0) AS mfa_success_rate
FROM ibm_verify.mfa_activity
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
  -- exclude "sent" rows; success rate is calculated from outcome events only.
  -- including "sent" would inflate the denominator and deflate the rate.
  AND result IN ('success', 'failure');
By week:
SELECT
    date_trunc('week', from_date) AS week_start,
    CAST(SUM(count) FILTER (WHERE result = 'success') AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(SUM(count), 0) AS mfa_success_rate
FROM ibm_verify.mfa_activity
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
  -- exclude "sent" rows; success rate is calculated from outcome events only.
  -- including "sent" would inflate the denominator and deflate the rate.
  AND result IN ('success', 'failure')
-- date_trunc('week', ...) anchors each bin to its Monday
GROUP BY date_trunc('week', from_date)
ORDER BY week_start;


Overall:
tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "mfa_activity")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28"),
    # exclude "sent" rows; success rate is calculated from outcome events only.
    # including "sent" would inflate the denominator and deflate the rate.
    result %in% c("success", "failure")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    mfa_success_rate =
      sum(if_else(result == "success", as.double(count), 0), na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(count, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
By week:
tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "mfa_activity")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28"),
    # exclude "sent" rows; success rate is calculated from outcome events only.
    # including "sent" would inflate the denominator and deflate the rate.
    result %in% c("success", "failure")
  ) |>
  # floor_date(..., "week") translates to Athena's date_trunc('week', ...),
  # which anchors each bin to its Monday
  mutate(week_start = floor_date(from_date, "week")) |>
  group_by(week_start) |>
  summarise(
    mfa_success_rate =
      sum(if_else(result == "success", as.double(count), 0), na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(count, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(week_start)



Interpretation notes. The rate is a proportion between 0 and 1. Interpret per factor where possible: a low overall rate may be driven by a single struggling factor rather than a system-wide problem. In the weekly view, the first and last weeks of the reporting window are usually partial (the window rarely starts or ends on a Monday), so treat boundary weeks with caution when comparing trends.


MFA Success Rate by Factor
The MFA success rate broken down by factor type, so that factors can be compared against one another.
This metric is useful for spotting whether one second factor is notably easier to clear than another. Because the rate is calculated per attempt, a factor that users frequently mistype before getting right (common with OTP factors) shows a lower rate, which is the friction signal you want when comparing ease of use.
Alternative names:

MFA success rate by method
MFA pass rate by method
second-factor success rate by factor
MFA completion rate by factor

Calculation. Within each mfa_type, divide the sum of count where result = 'success' by the sum of count where result is 'success' or 'failure'. As with the overall MFA Success Rate, sent rows are excluded from both the numerator and the denominator because they are not outcomes. mfa_attempts is the denominator (successes plus failures) and is reported alongside the rate so that low-volume factors, whose rates are noisy, can be discounted.
Note that mfa_attempts here counts success and failure events only. It is not the de-duplicated usage volume reported by All MFA Activity by Factor, which counts sent events for OTP factors instead.

SQLR


By factor:
SELECT
    mfa_type,
    SUM(count) AS mfa_attempts,
    CAST(SUM(count) FILTER (WHERE result = 'success') AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(SUM(count), 0) AS mfa_success_rate
FROM ibm_verify.mfa_activity
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
  -- exclude "sent" rows; success rate is calculated from outcome events only.
  -- including "sent" would inflate the denominator and deflate the rate.
  AND result IN ('success', 'failure')
GROUP BY mfa_type
ORDER BY mfa_attempts DESC;
By factor and week:
SELECT
    date_trunc('week', from_date) AS week_start,
    mfa_type,
    SUM(count) AS mfa_attempts,
    CAST(SUM(count) FILTER (WHERE result = 'success') AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(SUM(count), 0) AS mfa_success_rate
FROM ibm_verify.mfa_activity
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
  AND result IN ('success', 'failure')
-- date_trunc('week', ...) anchors each bin to its Monday
GROUP BY date_trunc('week', from_date), mfa_type
ORDER BY week_start, mfa_type;


By factor:
tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "mfa_activity")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28"),
    # exclude "sent" rows; success rate is calculated from outcome events only.
    # including "sent" would inflate the denominator and deflate the rate.
    result %in% c("success", "failure")
  ) |>
  group_by(mfa_type) |>
  summarise(
    mfa_attempts = sum(count, na.rm = TRUE),
    mfa_success_rate =
      sum(if_else(result == "success", as.double(count), 0), na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(count, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(mfa_attempts))
By factor and week:
tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "mfa_activity")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28"),
    result %in% c("success", "failure")
  ) |>
  # floor_date(..., "week") translates to Athena's date_trunc('week', ...),
  # which anchors each bin to its Monday
  mutate(week_start = floor_date(from_date, "week")) |>
  group_by(week_start, mfa_type) |>
  summarise(
    mfa_attempts = sum(count, na.rm = TRUE),
    mfa_success_rate =
      sum(if_else(result == "success", as.double(count), 0), na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(count, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(week_start, mfa_type)



Interpretation notes. mfa_success_rate is a proportion between 0 and 1. Always read it next to mfa_attempts: a factor with only a handful of attempts can post an extreme rate that reflects noise rather than ease of use. In the weekly view, the first and last weeks of the reporting window are usually partial (the window rarely starts or ends on a Monday), so treat boundary weeks with caution when comparing trends.


All MFA Activity by Factor
The volume of MFA attempts broken down by factor type, with each factor’s share of total activity. This is the platform-wide view: it covers every factor type the system records, including the internal and administrative factors (kq, totp, qr, ibm_verify_push) and the email_otp factor used only to validate emails at sign-up. For usage restricted to the factors the public can sign in with, see Sign-in MFA Activity by Factor.
This metric shows which second factors are exercised across the whole platform. Historically the mix has been dominated by sms_otp and email_otp. Passkeys (fido2) became generally available to the public on 2026-07-06, so fido2 volume is expected to grow from that point, while factors such as totp, kq, qr, voice_otp, and ibm_verify_push continue to see little use.
Alternative names:

MFA factor mix
second-factor distribution
MFA usage by method
MFA factor share

Calculation. Sum count grouped by mfa_type, then divide each factor’s total by the overall total to get its share. As with MFA Authentications, de-duplicate before counting: OTP factor types (sms_otp, email_otp, voice_otp) record both a sent event and a subsequent success/failure event for the same challenge, so only the sent row is counted for these; non-OTP factor types (fido2, kq, totp, ibm_verify_push) have no sent event and are counted through their success/failure rows. Without this, OTP factors are roughly double-counted relative to non-OTP factors.

SQLR


SELECT
    mfa_type,
    SUM(count) AS mfa_authentications,
    CAST(SUM(count) AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(SUM(SUM(count)) OVER (), 0) AS factor_share
FROM ibm_verify.mfa_activity
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
  -- de-duplicate OTP challenges: count only "sent" for OTP types, and
  -- "success"/"failure" for non-OTP types (which have no "sent" event).
  AND (
    result = 'sent'
    OR (
      result IN ('success', 'failure')
      AND mfa_type NOT IN ('sms_otp', 'email_otp', 'voice_otp')
    )
  )
GROUP BY mfa_type
ORDER BY mfa_authentications DESC;


otp_types <- c("sms_otp", "email_otp", "voice_otp")

tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "mfa_activity")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28"),
    # de-duplicate OTP challenges: count only "sent" for OTP types, and
    # "success"/"failure" for non-OTP types (which have no "sent" event).
    result == "sent" |
      (result %in% c("success", "failure") & !mfa_type %in% otp_types)
  ) |>
  group_by(mfa_type) |>
  summarise(
    mfa_authentications = sum(count, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    factor_share =
      as.double(mfa_authentications) / sum(mfa_authentications, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(mfa_authentications))



Interpretation notes. factor_share is a proportion between 0 and 1 and sums to 1 across all factors. Combine with the MFA success rate to spot factors that are both heavily used and failing often.


Sign-in MFA Activity by Factor
The volume of MFA attempts across the factors the public can use to sign in, with each factor’s share of the total.
Where All MFA Activity by Factor covers every factor type the platform records, this metric keeps only the ones a member of the public can present as a second factor when signing in: sms_otp, voice_otp, and, since passkeys became generally available on 2026-07-06, fido2. The internal and administrative factors that dominate the platform-wide view are left out.
Two factors that reach the public are deliberately excluded:

email_otp is offered only to validate an email address at sign-up; it cannot be used to sign in, so counting it here would overstate sign-in factor usage.
kq, totp, qr, and ibm_verify_push are used internally or for testing, not part of the public sign-in flow.

Alternative names:

sign-in factor mix
public sign-in factor share
sign-in-capable factor distribution

Calculation. Restrict to the sign-in factors (sms_otp, voice_otp, fido2), then, as in All MFA Activity by Factor, de-duplicate before counting: the OTP factors (sms_otp, voice_otp) record both a sent event and a subsequent success/failure event for the same challenge, so only the sent row is counted for these; fido2 (passkeys) has no sent event and is counted through its success/failure rows. Sum count grouped by mfa_type, then divide each factor’s total by the combined total to get its share.

SQLR


SELECT
    mfa_type,
    SUM(count) AS mfa_authentications,
    CAST(SUM(count) AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(SUM(SUM(count)) OVER (), 0) AS factor_share
FROM ibm_verify.mfa_activity
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
  -- restrict to the factors the public can use to sign in. email_otp is
  -- excluded because it only validates emails at sign-up, and kq, totp, qr,
  -- and ibm_verify_push are internal factors, not public sign-in options.
  AND mfa_type IN ('sms_otp', 'voice_otp', 'fido2')
  -- de-duplicate OTP challenges: count only "sent" for the OTP factors
  -- (sms_otp, voice_otp), and "success"/"failure" for fido2 (passkeys),
  -- which has no "sent" event.
  AND (
    result = 'sent'
    OR (
      result IN ('success', 'failure')
      AND mfa_type NOT IN ('sms_otp', 'email_otp', 'voice_otp')
    )
  )
GROUP BY mfa_type
ORDER BY mfa_authentications DESC;


otp_types <- c("sms_otp", "email_otp", "voice_otp")
sign_in_types <- c("sms_otp", "voice_otp", "fido2")

tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "mfa_activity")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28"),
    # restrict to the factors the public can use to sign in. email_otp is
    # excluded because it only validates emails at sign-up, and kq, totp, qr,
    # and ibm_verify_push are internal factors, not public sign-in options.
    mfa_type %in% sign_in_types,
    # de-duplicate OTP challenges: count only "sent" for the OTP factors
    # (sms_otp, voice_otp), and "success"/"failure" for fido2 (passkeys),
    # which has no "sent" event.
    result == "sent" |
      (result %in% c("success", "failure") & !mfa_type %in% otp_types)
  ) |>
  group_by(mfa_type) |>
  summarise(
    mfa_authentications = sum(count, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    factor_share =
      as.double(mfa_authentications) / sum(mfa_authentications, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(mfa_authentications))



Interpretation notes. factor_share is a proportion between 0 and 1 and sums to 1 across these factors only, so it is not comparable to the shares in All MFA Activity by Factor, which are taken over every factor. Passkey (fido2) activity appears only from the 2026-07-06 rollout onward, so a reporting window that predates that date will show little or none.

title: Jira (PSOM)
text:
Metrics in this section are calculated from PSOM Jira Service Management ticket data.
The relevant Jira table is:
Data cadence. The jira.psom table is updated once per week (every Monday at ~06:00 ET). Each run writes a full snapshot of all current tickets to a new date= partition; previous partitions are preserved as an audit trail. Always filter to date = (SELECT MAX(date) FROM jira.psom) when querying the latest state.

title: Jira (PSOM)
text:
PSOM Metrics

Tickets by Status
The number of open PSOM Jira tickets grouped by their current workflow status, based on the latest weekly snapshot.
Alternative names:

ticket status breakdown
open tickets by status
PSOM ticket status distribution

Calculation. Filter to the most recent date partition and count tickets by status.

SQLR


SELECT
    status,
    COUNT(*) AS ticket_count
FROM jira.psom
WHERE date = (SELECT MAX(date) FROM jira.psom)
GROUP BY status
ORDER BY ticket_count DESC;


psom <- tbl(con, in_schema("jira", "psom"))
latest_date <- psom |> summarise(d = max(date, na.rm = TRUE)) |> pull(d)

psom |>
  filter(as.Date(date) == as.Date(latest_date)) |>
  count(status, name = "ticket_count") |>
  arrange(desc(ticket_count))





Weekly Ticket Volume
The total number of PSOM tickets captured in each weekly snapshot, showing how the backlog size changes over time.
Alternative names:

total tickets per week
weekly PSOM ticket count
ticket volume trend

Calculation. Count the number of rows per date partition. Each partition is one Monday snapshot, so the result is one total per week.

SQLR


SELECT
    date,
    COUNT(*) AS ticket_count
FROM jira.psom
GROUP BY date
ORDER BY date DESC;


tbl(con, in_schema("jira", "psom")) |>
  count(date, name = "ticket_count") |>
  arrange(desc(date))





Tickets by Priority
The number of PSOM tickets in the latest snapshot grouped by priority level.
Alternative names:

ticket priority breakdown
PSOM tickets by priority
priority distribution

Calculation. Filter to the most recent date partition and count tickets by priority.

SQLR


SELECT
    priority,
    COUNT(*) AS ticket_count
FROM jira.psom
WHERE date = (SELECT MAX(date) FROM jira.psom)
GROUP BY priority
ORDER BY ticket_count DESC;


psom <- tbl(con, in_schema("jira", "psom"))
latest_date <- psom |> summarise(d = max(date, na.rm = TRUE)) |> pull(d)

psom |>
  filter(as.Date(date) == as.Date(latest_date)) |>
  count(priority, name = "ticket_count") |>
  arrange(desc(ticket_count))





SLA First-Response Breach Rate
The proportion of PSOM tickets that missed the “Time to first response” SLA (a 4-hour response target), based on the latest weekly snapshot.
This metric is useful for monitoring whether the team is meeting initial response commitments. Only tickets where the SLA clock was active are included; tickets with no SLA (e.g., non-service-request issue types or tickets created before the SLA scheme was configured) are excluded.
Alternative names:

first response SLA breach rate
time to first response SLA compliance
first-response SLA failure rate

Calculation. Filter to the most recent date partition and to tickets where sla_first_response_breached is not empty (i.e., the SLA was active). Divide the count of tickets where sla_first_response_breached = 'True' by the total count of SLA-active tickets.

SQLR


SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_sla_tickets,
    SUM(CASE WHEN sla_first_response_breached = 'True' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS breached_count,
    CAST(SUM(CASE WHEN sla_first_response_breached = 'True' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) AS breach_rate
FROM jira.psom
WHERE date = (SELECT MAX(date) FROM jira.psom)
  AND sla_first_response_breached != '';


psom <- tbl(con, in_schema("jira", "psom"))
latest_date <- psom |> summarise(d = max(date, na.rm = TRUE)) |> pull(d)

psom |>
  filter(as.Date(date) == as.Date(latest_date), sla_first_response_breached != "") |>
  summarise(
    total_sla_tickets = n(),
    breached_count    = sum(as.integer(sla_first_response_breached == "True"), na.rm = TRUE),
    breach_rate       = sum(as.integer(sla_first_response_breached == "True"), na.rm = TRUE) /
                          as.double(n())
  )



Interpretation notes. The rate is a proportion between 0 and 1. Tickets with no active SLA (empty sla_first_response_breached) are excluded from both numerator and denominator. Check total_sla_tickets to understand the sample size underlying the rate.
A ticket that is still open and within its 4-hour window is recorded as not breached ("False"), the same as a ticket that met the SLA. The rate on a live snapshot is therefore a lower bound: some of those open tickets may still breach before they are answered. It is most meaningful once the snapshot’s tickets are largely resolved.


SLA Time-to-Done Breach Rate
The proportion of PSOM tickets that missed the “Time to done” SLA (a 16-hour resolution target), based on the latest weekly snapshot.
This metric reflects how well the team is resolving tickets within the agreed timeframe. Only tickets where the SLA clock was active are included; tickets with no SLA configuration are excluded.
Alternative names:

time to done SLA breach rate
resolution SLA compliance
time-to-done SLA failure rate

Calculation. Filter to the most recent date partition and to tickets where sla_time_to_done_breached is not empty (i.e., the SLA was active). Divide the count of tickets where sla_time_to_done_breached = 'True' by the total count of SLA-active tickets.

SQLR


SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_sla_tickets,
    SUM(CASE WHEN sla_time_to_done_breached = 'True' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS breached_count,
    CAST(SUM(CASE WHEN sla_time_to_done_breached = 'True' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) AS breach_rate
FROM jira.psom
WHERE date = (SELECT MAX(date) FROM jira.psom)
  AND sla_time_to_done_breached != '';


psom <- tbl(con, in_schema("jira", "psom"))
latest_date <- psom |> summarise(d = max(date, na.rm = TRUE)) |> pull(d)

psom |>
  filter(as.Date(date) == as.Date(latest_date), sla_time_to_done_breached != "") |>
  summarise(
    total_sla_tickets = n(),
    breached_count    = sum(as.integer(sla_time_to_done_breached == "True"), na.rm = TRUE),
    breach_rate       = sum(as.integer(sla_time_to_done_breached == "True"), na.rm = TRUE) /
                          as.double(n())
  )



Interpretation notes. The rate is a proportion between 0 and 1. Tickets with no active SLA (empty sla_time_to_done_breached) are excluded from both numerator and denominator. Check total_sla_tickets to understand the sample size underlying the rate.
A ticket that is still open and within its 16-hour window is recorded as not breached ("False"), the same as a ticket that met the SLA. The rate on a live snapshot is therefore a lower bound: some of those open tickets may still breach before they are resolved. It is most meaningful once the snapshot’s tickets are largely resolved.

title: Platform
text:
Site reliability metrics for the CanadaLogin platform, in the sre schema. Currently incident-response metrics, with other reliability measures to follow.
The relevant tables are:
These tables come from a hand-maintained spreadsheet; see the catalog Data quality note for caveats. The one that shapes most queries here: the incident timestamps (detected_at, resolved_at, and so on) are stored as America/Toronto strings, not native dates, so a reporting window filters on the YYYY-MM-DD prefix of detected_at rather than a from_date column.

title: Platform
text:
Incident Response Metrics

Mean Time to Resolution
The average time, in minutes, from when an incident is detected to when it is fully resolved.
Alternative names:

MTTR
time to resolve
incident resolution time
mean time to recovery (see the interpretation note on this overloaded term)

Calculation. Average mttr_detected_resolved (minutes from detected_at to resolved_at, precomputed in the table) across the incidents whose detected_at falls in the reporting window, from sre.mttr_current. Because the incident timestamps are stored as America/Toronto strings rather than native dates, the window casts the YYYY-MM-DD prefix of detected_at to a DATE instead of filtering a from_date column. Unresolved incidents have a null mttr_detected_resolved and are excluded from the average.
To break the figure out for service-level reporting, group the same average by severity.

SQLR


Overall:
SELECT
    AVG(mttr_detected_resolved) AS mean_time_to_resolution
FROM sre.mttr_current
-- set the reporting window on detected_at; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE CAST(substr(detected_at, 1, 10) AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-06-26';
By severity:
SELECT
    severity,
    AVG(mttr_detected_resolved) AS mean_time_to_resolution
FROM sre.mttr_current
-- set the reporting window on detected_at; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE CAST(substr(detected_at, 1, 10) AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-06-26'
GROUP BY severity
ORDER BY severity;


Overall:
tbl(con, in_schema("sre", "mttr_current")) |>
  # set the reporting window on detected_at; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    as.Date(str_sub(detected_at, 1, 10)) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(str_sub(detected_at, 1, 10)) <= as.Date("2026-06-26")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    mean_time_to_resolution = mean(mttr_detected_resolved, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
By severity:
tbl(con, in_schema("sre", "mttr_current")) |>
  # set the reporting window on detected_at; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    as.Date(str_sub(detected_at, 1, 10)) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(str_sub(detected_at, 1, 10)) <= as.Date("2026-06-26")
  ) |>
  group_by(severity) |>
  summarise(
    mean_time_to_resolution = mean(mttr_detected_resolved, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(severity)



Interpretation notes. The figure is in minutes; divide by 60 for hours. It is a mean, so a single very long incident can pull it up sharply, especially given how few incidents there are in any window. Read it alongside the incident count, and consider a median or a Sev1-only cut when one outlier dominates.
The window is anchored on detected_at, so an incident counts in the period it began, even if it was resolved in a later period. severity is unvalidated free text in the source sheet, so the grouped view reflects whatever values were typed (expect the occasional typo or spacing drift).

title: Platform
text:
Site Uptime Metrics
Uptime and response-time metrics for the six monitored CanadaLogin sites, in the sre schema. Data comes from the cds-snc/status-statut GitHub repository and is fetched automatically by the uptime-daily-download Lambda at 04:00 ET each day. No manual steps are required.
The relevant tables are:

sre.uptime_summary - one row per site per day; rolling-window uptime percentages and average response times
sre.uptime_timeseries - one row per upptime bot commit per site; individual ping events including outage detections

Both tables are partitioned by date=YYYY-MM-DD. Because date is a varchar (derived from the Hive partition path), cast it before doing date arithmetic: CAST(date AS DATE) in SQL; as.Date(date) in R.

Site Uptime
The rolling-window uptime percentage for each monitored CanadaLogin site, showing what proportion of five-minute pings succeeded over each time horizon.
Six sites are monitored: the two public-facing login pages (EN/FR), the Manage Profile app (staging and production), and the IdP (staging and production).
Alternative names:

uptime percentage
availability percentage
site availability
service uptime by site

Calculation. Filter to the most recent date partition and read the precomputed rolling-window uptime columns from sre.uptime_summary. Each uptime_* column is already a percentage (0-100), calculated from all five-minute pings over the named window.
To track uptime over time for a specific site, remove the MAX(date) filter and group by date.

SQLR


Latest snapshot, all sites, all rolling windows:
SELECT
    slug,
    lang,
    status,
    uptime_day,
    uptime_week,
    uptime_month,
    uptime_year,
    uptime_alltime
FROM sre.uptime_summary
WHERE date = (SELECT MAX(date) FROM sre.uptime_summary)
ORDER BY slug;
Trend over time, year uptime for a specific site:
SELECT
    date,
    uptime_year,
    uptime_alltime
FROM sre.uptime_summary
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE slug = 'canada-login-website-en'
  AND CAST(date AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-06-30'
ORDER BY date;


Latest snapshot, all sites, all rolling windows:
uptime_s <- tbl(con, in_schema("sre", "uptime_summary"))
latest_date <- uptime_s |> summarise(d = max(date, na.rm = TRUE)) |> pull(d)

uptime_s |>
  filter(as.Date(date) == as.Date(latest_date)) |>
  select(slug, lang, status, uptime_day, uptime_week, uptime_month,
         uptime_year, uptime_alltime) |>
  arrange(slug)
Trend over time, year uptime for a specific site:
tbl(con, in_schema("sre", "uptime_summary")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    slug == "canada-login-website-en",
    as.Date(date) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date) <= as.Date("2026-06-30")
  ) |>
  select(date, uptime_year, uptime_alltime) |>
  arrange(date)



Interpretation notes. The uptime windows are rolling, not calendar-aligned. uptime_month on a given date covers the 30 days trailing that date’s stats update (~23:2x UTC), not a fixed calendar month. To aggregate by calendar month, group by substr(date, 1, 7).


Site Response Time
The average HTTP response time for each monitored CanadaLogin site, aggregated over rolling windows from five-minute ping data.
Alternative names:

average response time
site latency
response latency by site
HTTP response time

Calculation. Filter to the most recent date partition and read the precomputed rolling-window response time columns from sre.uptime_summary. Each response_*_ms column is the mean response time in milliseconds over all five-minute pings in the named window.
For a single-ping point-in-time reading (not an average), use sre.uptime_timeseries.response_ms instead.

SQLR


Latest snapshot, all sites, all rolling windows:
SELECT
    slug,
    lang,
    status,
    response_day_ms,
    response_week_ms,
    response_month_ms,
    response_year_ms,
    response_alltime_ms
FROM sre.uptime_summary
WHERE date = (SELECT MAX(date) FROM sre.uptime_summary)
ORDER BY slug;
Trend over time, weekly average response time for a specific site:
SELECT
    date,
    response_week_ms,
    response_alltime_ms
FROM sre.uptime_summary
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE slug = 'canada-login-website-en'
  AND CAST(date AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-06-30'
ORDER BY date;


Latest snapshot, all sites, all rolling windows:
uptime_s <- tbl(con, in_schema("sre", "uptime_summary"))
latest_date <- uptime_s |> summarise(d = max(date, na.rm = TRUE)) |> pull(d)

uptime_s |>
  filter(as.Date(date) == as.Date(latest_date)) |>
  select(slug, lang, status, response_day_ms, response_week_ms,
         response_month_ms, response_year_ms, response_alltime_ms) |>
  arrange(slug)
Trend over time, weekly average response time for a specific site:
tbl(con, in_schema("sre", "uptime_summary")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    slug == "canada-login-website-en",
    as.Date(date) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date) <= as.Date("2026-06-30")
  ) |>
  select(date, response_week_ms, response_alltime_ms) |>
  arrange(date)



Interpretation notes. Response times are averages computed from all 288 five-minute pings in the window. Single-measurement spikes are smoothed out. During outage periods, pings that time out contribute high response times or are excluded depending on the upptime implementation, so treat response time trends during known outage windows with caution.


Outage Events
Individual site outage events detected by the uptime monitor: when a site went down, when it recovered, and the HTTP status code at time of detection.
Alternative names:

downtime events
site outages
availability incidents
uptime incidents

Calculation. Filter sre.uptime_timeseries to rows where status = 'down'. Each such row represents a moment when the upptime bot detected the site was unreachable and committed that state to the history file.
To count outage events per site over a reporting period, group the filtered rows by slug and count.

SQLR


All outage events in a reporting period:
SELECT
    date,
    ping_timestamp,
    slug,
    lang,
    http_code,
    response_ms
FROM sre.uptime_timeseries
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE status = 'down'
  AND CAST(date AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-06-30'
ORDER BY date DESC, slug;
Outage event count per site in a reporting period:
SELECT
    slug,
    lang,
    COUNT(*) AS outage_event_count
FROM sre.uptime_timeseries
-- set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
WHERE status = 'down'
  AND CAST(date AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-06-30'
GROUP BY slug, lang
ORDER BY outage_event_count DESC;


All outage events in a reporting period:
tbl(con, in_schema("sre", "uptime_timeseries")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    status == "down",
    as.Date(date) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date) <= as.Date("2026-06-30")
  ) |>
  select(date, ping_timestamp, slug, lang, http_code, response_ms) |>
  arrange(desc(date), slug)
Outage event count per site in a reporting period:
tbl(con, in_schema("sre", "uptime_timeseries")) |>
  # set the reporting window; see the timespans note in cookbook/notes.qmd
  filter(
    status == "down",
    as.Date(date) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date) <= as.Date("2026-06-30")
  ) |>
  count(slug, lang, name = "outage_event_count") |>
  arrange(desc(outage_event_count))



Interpretation notes. Each row in sre.uptime_timeseries is a single commit by the upptime bot. On a normal all-up day there is one row per site (the daily ~23:2x UTC snapshot). On an outage day there are additional rows (one when the site went down and one when it recovered), so status = 'down' rows count detection events, not total minutes of downtime. For total outage duration, use the daily_minutes_down JSON field in sre.uptime_summary.
